coredata.dc:title: The photodegradation of naproxen in an aqueous solution employing a cobalt ferrite-carbon quantum dots (CF/N-CQDs) nanocomposite, synthesized via microwave approach
coredata.dc:creator: Mmelesi, Olga K.
Ammar-Merah, Souad
Nkambule, Thabo T.I.
Nkosi, Bongani
Liu, Xinying
Kefeni, Kebede K.
Kuvarega, Alex T.
coredata.dc:description: A microwave method was used to successfully synthesize magnetic photocatalytic CoFe2O4/N-Carbon quantum dots (CF/N-CQDs) composite. To assess the inherent qualities of the prepared nanomaterials, a comprehensive analysis was conducted employing various analytical techniques, including powder UV–visible diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS), infrared (IR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–vis and PL results showed that the addition of CF and N-CQDs improved the optical properties. A superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) was used to measure the magnetic properties of CF and CF/N-CQDs composites at room temperature and they showed superparamagnetic behavior. The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanomaterials was tested in an aqueous medium while exposed to visible light using naproxen (NPX) as a model pollutant. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CF/N-CQDs composite was found to be the highest, as compared to the pristine materials. Quenching experiments revealed that the photocatalytic degradation process involved HO, h+, and O2 −. A potential photocatalytic mechanism was also proposed, and the composite demonstrated high reusability in four cycles due to its ferromagnetic properties.

head.ce:title: The photodegradation of naproxen in an aqueous solution employing a cobalt ferrite-carbon quantum dots (CF/N-CQDs) nanocomposite, synthesized via microwave approach

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A microwave method was used to successfully synthesize magnetic photocatalytic CoFe2O4/N-Carbon quantum dots (CF/N-CQDs) composite. To assess the inherent qualities of the prepared nanomaterials, a comprehensive analysis was conducted employing various analytical techniques, including powder UV–visible diffuse reflection spectroscopy (DRS), infrared (IR) spectroscopy, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), X-ray diffraction (XRD), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The UV–vis and PL results showed that the addition of CF and N-CQDs improved the optical properties. A superconducting quantum interference device (SQUID) was used to measure the magnetic properties of CF and CF/N-CQDs composites at room temperature and they showed superparamagnetic behavior. The photocatalytic performance of the synthesized nanomaterials was tested in an aqueous medium while exposed to visible light using naproxen (NPX) as a model pollutant. The photocatalytic efficiency of the CF/N-CQDs composite was found to be the highest, as compared to the pristine materials. Quenching experiments revealed that the photocatalytic degradation process involved HO, h+, and O2 −. A potential photocatalytic mechanism was also proposed, and the composite demonstrated high reusability in four cycles due to its ferromagnetic properties.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The need to prioritize public health has led to the mass manufacturing and usage of pharmaceuticals [1,2]. However, these drugs are only partially metabolized upon ingestion, leaving behind unchanged remnants that find their way into water sources through different channels [3]. Additionally, medical waste, agricultural waste, household sewage, and industrial wastewater effluent are all sources of significant pharmaceutical discharge into water resources. This includes outdated medications that retain their original form [4,5]. Naproxen (NPX) is a valuable non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug (NSAID) used for its analgesic properties [6]. As a member of the aryl acetic acid group, NPX has an empirical formula of C14H14O3 and a molecular weight of 230.26 g/mol. It's known for its antipyretic and analgesic effects, making it a preferred treatment option for skeletal pain, inflammation caused by menstrual cramps and arthritis [7]. Despite its low concentration (ng/L to μg/L) in the ecosystem, the continuous inflow of wasted NPX into water system makes it a recalcitrant pollutant that harms aquatic life and the ecosystem [8]. To effectively address the issue of NPX wastage in water, it is essential to focus research efforts on developing methods that can either remove or degrade these pollutants. Conventional methods for eradicating pharmaceutical pollutants frequently prove inadequate owing to their inherent resistance to biodegradation and their propensity to generate more deleterious byproducts. Unfortunately, established chemical and physical treatment methods, such as activated carbon adsorption, chemical separation, filtration, and reverse osmosis, are not only expensive but also require significant amounts of energy [9]. Additionally, these methods are often unable to completely remove contaminants and can even concentrate them, leading to the formation of additional harmful byproducts that require further treatment [10]. Therefore, it is critical that research is directed towards finding more effective and sustainable solutions to this pressing issue. Utilizing nanotechnology, environmental remediation can be achieved through photocatalytic strategies that take advantage of abundant light sources [11]. Photocatalysis has become increasingly popular for removing organic pollutants and industrial effluents, while simultaneously completely mineralizing them into CO2 and H2O, leaving behind no by-products [12,13]. To effectively degrade pollutants, a variety of semiconductor catalysts have been utilized. Metal oxides, sulphides, and nitrides, as well as metal-free semiconductors, are frequently employed in these applications. Metal oxides are highly sought-after as semiconductor catalysts due to their various advantages [13]. Magnetic nanoparticles are also effective in the organic pollutants degradation process because of their surface-to-volume ratio and adsorption properties. Ferrites, in particular, have shown great promise as photocatalysts for breaking down dyes and natural pollutants [14]. Ferrites are typically categorized as spinel, garnet, hexaferrite, and orthoferrite. Among these, spinel ferrites are the most sought-after magnetic nanoparticles because of their simple chemical composition and superior magnetic properties. They are widely used in biomedical, electronic devices, catalysis, and wastewater treatment applications [15]. CoFe2O4 NPs are particularly appealing due to their fundamental properties, including their physical toughness, chemical stability, ability to absorb visible light, low band gap energy (2.04 eV), and high saturation magnetization (Ms) [16,17]. However, pure CoFe2O4 NPs tend to experience irreversible aggregation due to their large surface area and high electron-hole recombination, thus low photocatalytic efficiency [18]. To address these issues, several approaches have been developed, including dispersing aggregated NPs onto different supports, particularly those based on carbon [19]. This method has been shown to be especially effective in increasing the photocatalytic activity of CoFe2O4 NPs. Moradi et al. sought to improve wastewater treatment by combining the UVC/persulfate method with spinel cobalt ferrite (SCF) nanostructures anchored with graphitic carbon nitride in their study (g-C3N4, GCN). After five cycles of catalyst reuse, the system successfully removed more than 95 % of BPA, indicating the potential cost-effectiveness of SCF@GCN's reusability. Additionally, the study discovered a S-scheme heterojunction mechanism for the transfer of photo-generated electron/hole pairs in the SCF@GCN catalyst [20]. Furthermore, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have a number of distinct attributes, including high chemical inertness, ease of functionalization, nontoxicity, dependable stability, and efficient electron deposits and transport capabilities [21]. CQDs have the ability to assist electrons in the composite interface, which leads to improved movement of electrons and catalyst stability when combined with metals and carbon [22]. The photocatalytic method allows for direct absorption of photons by the CQDs, which induces e−/h+ pairs and transforms them into effective photocatalysts [23]. CQDs enhance semiconductor photocatalysis by creating a heterojunction that separates photogenerated charge carriers, increasing the photocatalytic conversion due to their wide spectrum light absorption, and adsorbing organic pollutants through their conjugated π structure [24]. CQDs are even more effective in the photocatalytic process due to the presence of functional groups like carboxyl, hydroxyl, and amine. This is because they are able to easily respond to visible light because of n-π* interactions [25,26]. Metal semiconductors are often plagued by photo-corrosion under light irradiation, which is a major disadvantage. To combat this issue, constructing heterojunctions with other semiconductors, particularly carbon quantum dots, is the primary solution. Bi7O9I3/g-C3N4 (BOI/CN) composites, incorporating lignin-derived carbon quantum dots (CQDs), were synthesized using a hydrothermal method and subsequently employed for the simultaneous photocatalytic degradation of Cr(VI) and levofloxacin (LEV). The 0.5-CQD/BOI/CN photocatalyst, featuring a 0.5 % CQD content, demonstrated enhanced light-harvesting capability, superior charge separation efficiency, and accelerated electron transfer. Notably, this catalyst exhibited a remarkable photocatalytic performance, achieving 100 % removal efficiency for Cr(VI) and 94.8 % for LEV [27]. Similarly, this research study produced a new type of nanocomposite material using a straightforward hydrothermal process. The material, composed of boron-doped CQD (BCQD) and g-C3N4 heterojunction (CN), demonstrated exceptional performance. The most efficient sample, 1-BCQD/CN (with a boron to CQD mass ratio of 1:3), achieved 96.8 % rhodamine photodegradation performance in just 4 h through an 18 W LED light irradiation. This credit belongs to a plethora of variables, including improved electron charge transfer from CN to BCQDs, lower electron-hole pair recombination rates, and higher charge density, which facilitates charge migration [28]. Considering that the morphology and surface properties of nanocomposites can change significantly with the synthesis procedure, it is inevitable that the synthesis method could have an effect on the photocatalytic activities of the synthesized nanoparticles. In this manuscript, CF/N-CQDs heterojunction was prepared via in-situ microwave method, which is known as a simple, fast, effective, and environmentally friendly method for the large-scale production of metallic nanoparticles. Structure, morphology, magnetic, optical attributes, and chemical composition of the photocatalysts were investigated. Photocatalytic activity was evaluated using NPX as the model pollutant.

Iron nitrate nonahydrate (98.0 %), cobalt nitrate hexahydrate (99 %), triethylene glycol with 99 % purity; tertbutyl alcohol, isopropanol (99.9 %), ascorbic acid (99 %), urea, and citric acid, were bought from Sigma Aldrich. Methylparaben (99 %), ethanol, Benzoquinone (BQ, 96 %), nitro-blue tetrazolium (NBT, 98 %). All the chemicals were used without further treatment. The chemicals acquired from Sigma Aldrich (South Africa), CC Immelmann, and ACROS.

The cobalt ferrite was synthesized by the polyol method previously reported in our manuscript [29], where 0.1 mol of Co2+ and 0.2 mol of Fe3+ were mixed gently in a 250 ml triethylene glycol solvent. The solution was heated from room temperature to boiling temperature (221 °C) at a rate of 6 °C/min, and the reaction was refluxed for 15 h. The CoFe2O4 NPs were washed with acetone several times, centrifuged to remove dissolved organics, and then air-dried overnight at 50 °C.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-situ microwave synthesis of CF/N-CQDs composite
ce:section.ce:para: Cobalt ferrite/N‑carbon quantum dots (CF/N-CQDs) nanocomposite was synthesized by microwave method. 1:1:1 mol ratios of CF: urea: citric acid was dispersed in 10 mL of water. The solution was sonicated for 15 min and then heated in a microwave for 5 min at 900 W. The black solid powder was collected for further use and characterization. And for the synthesis of N-CQDs 1:1 mol ratio of urea and citric acid were dissolved in H2O. Then the mixture was also subjected to microwave irradiation power of 900 W for 5 min. Then the solid brown powder was collected for and kept for further analysis. Proline, microwave frequency of 50 Hz to 2.45 GHz (domestic microwave) was used.

The Rigaku Smart Lab X-ray Diffractometer (XRD) employing Cu – Ka radiation was utilized to investigate the phase structure within the 2θ range of 20–80°. Fourier transform infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained using the PerkinElmer FTIR Spectrometer Frontier within the 4000–400 cm−1 range. The PerkinElmer UV/Vis/NIR spectrophotometer Lambda 1050 was employed for recording diffuse reflectance. The determination of the bandgap energy of the ferrite nanoparticles was conducted through the examination of Tauc plots. Photoluminescence (PL) analysis, with an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, was carried out using the Horiba Fluorolog-3 Jobnyvon. All the nanomaterials were used as the working electrode, Ag/AgCl as the reference electrode and platinum wire as the counter electrode in 0.1 M KCl solution. The electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was performed using the same electrochemical workstation (BioLogic SAS, SP-50e) in the frequency range from 1 KHz to 0.01 mHz. Then, the photocatalyst was dispersed in water and then ultrasonicated for 10 min to form a slurry solution. The slurry was dip-coated onto a precleaned working electrode (Glassy carbon electrode). After air-drying, the prepared photoelectrode was immersed in a 3 mM ferric ferrocyanide (FCC) electrolyte solution. The magnetic properties of the samples were evaluated through a Quantum Design MPMS-5S (SQUID) analysis. The thermal variance of magnetic susceptibility for each sample across a temperature range of 5 to 330 K was analyzed. Both FC and ZFC conditions were tested, utilizing a DC magnetic field of 200 Oe. Subsequently, the ZFC isothermal variance of magnetization in relation to the magnetic field, at temperatures of 5 and 300 K was observed. The ESCALAB 250 (Thermo Scientific – VG) and K-Alpha + (ThermoFisher Scientific) spectrometers were used to measure the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) of the pristine N-CQDs, CoFe2O4 and composite nanomaterial. Additionally, the thermogravimetric analysis was conducted using the ATG/ATD Labsys Evo device from SETARAM of THERMOSCIENTIFIC. This analysis was carried out at a heating rate of 10 °C/min in the presence of helium gas, ranging from room temperature to 800 °C.

For the experimental procedure, 0.02 g of the photocatalyst was introduced into a 100 mL solution containing pollutants at a concentration of 10 mg/L within the photoreactor. Prior to activating the lamp, the slurry underwent stirring at 650 rpm for 20 min to accomplish adsorption-desorption balance. Samples of 2 mL were taken at 20-min periods and filtered via a 0.45 micropores syringe filter for further analysis. The investigation explored the impact of various parameters, including pH, pollutant concentration, and different water matrices. In addition, radical scavengers were employed to elucidate the radicals generated during the photodegradation procedures. Radical probes, isopropanol (ISO), Formic acid (FA), and ascorbic acid (AA), were used to differentiate the roles of OH, h+, and O2 − in the degradation of NPX. The concentration of each scavenger was kept at 5 mM. To determine the effectiveness of degrading the pollutant, the equation below was utilized.

Ct denotes the concentration at a specific time, C0 indicates the initial concentration, k represents the observed rate constant, and t indicates time.

As depicted in Fig. 1a, the crystal structures of N-CQDs, cobalt ferrite (CF), and CF/N-CQDs composites were examined using X-ray diffraction (XRD). The XRD patterns reveal distinct peaks at 18.1°, 30.2°, 35.6°, 43.3°, 53.6°, 57.2°, and 62.7°, corresponding to the (111), (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) lattice planes of CF, as identified by the JCPDS card No. 22–1086 [30]. The X-ray XRD pattern, as illustrated in the inset Fig., exhibits a broad hump cantered at 2θ approximately 25°, indicative of the amorphous nature of carbon quantum dots. Nevertheless, it is noteworthy that this particular position is associated with the presence of a graphitic carbon structure, as evidenced by (002) diffraction [31]. Notably, in the CF/N-CQDs composites, the characteristic peak (002) attributable to N-CQDs was observed.

The samples underwent additional characterization through FT-IR spectroscopy to identify the surface's functional groups. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibit absorption peaks at 3413, 2926, 1704, and 1114 cm−1, which correlate to OH/NH, CH, CO/CC, and CN bonds, respectively, as shown in Fig. 1b. At 1644 cm−1, the broad absorption band of prepared N-CQDs corresponds to the character trait vibration of the CC (sp2) stretching mode [32,33]. The stretching vibration of the FeO and CoO bonds causes the absorption bands at 570 cm−1 and 420 cm−1 in pristine CF, respectively, and the wide absorption at 3416 cm−1 is caused by the OH bond [20]. N-CQD and CF characteristic peaks can be noticed in CF/N-CQD composites, demonstrating the successful development of the nanocomposite [34].

All samples were subjected to UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy to determine their optical absorption capacity, as shown in Fig. 2a. All samples clearly have a relatively broad absorption ability in the 200–800 nm range. When compared to pristine CF, the absorption capacity of the composite material did not change after the addition of N-CQDs, which is beneficial for utilizing advanced visible light to maximize photoactivity [35]. The band gap energies were determined by using Eq. (3) and extrapolating the straight-line portion of (αhv)2 on hv-axis as shown in Fig. 2b and Table S1, the bad gap energies were approximated to be 1.90–1.98 eV.
(3)
αhv
n / 2
= A
hv −
E g

For the modified Tauc plots estimation, the linear portion of the plot was identified, and this region corresponds to the indirect band gap. A straight line was fitted to the linear portion of the plot.

In addition, photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CF, N-CQDs, and CF/N-CQDs were shown in Fig. 2c to demonstrate the case of separation of the photo-generated carriers. Pure CF exhibits a strong PL emission peak at around 495 nm under 320 nm excitation wavelengths, and the PL peak intensity of pure CF is considerably higher than that of the CF/N-CQDs composite, illustrating that the inclusion of N-CQDs effectively enhances the effectiveness of photo-generated electron transfer, thus boosting photocatalytic activity [36]. Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) is a promising method for investigating the charge separation and transfer behavior of a photocatalyst. In general, a larger arc radius on the Nyquist circle indicates higher charge transfer resistance. Fig. 2c shows the Nyquist circle for CF, N-CQDs, and CF/N-CQDs photocatalysts. The larger arc radius of CF indicates a higher charge-transfer resistance than that of CF/N-CQDs and N-CQDs photocatalysts. The CF/N-CQDs photocatalyst had a smaller arc radius than pristine material. The results showed that loading N-CQDs significantly increased electron mobility, which reduces charge-transfer resistance. The EIS results were consistent with the findings from cyclic voltammetry. The CV curves in Fig. 2d revealed that the CF/N-CQDs nanocatalyst had a higher capacitive area and a longer charge-discharge time than the pristine materials, indicating that it performed better catalytically [37,38].

XPS was used to determine the surface chemical element composition of as-prepared samples, which is shown in Fig. 3 . In Fig. 3a, the comprehensive spectra of CF, N-CQDs, and CF/N-CQDs are shown, and it is clear that the four elements of C, Co, Fe, and O are present in the sample CF, implying that the sample was successfully synthesized with no contaminants. The occurrence of the C element in CF is unexpected, and it could be traced back to the adhesive tape used on the XPS instrument [39]. Additionally the N-CQDs nanomaterial, only C, N and O were detected. However, on the CF/N-CQDs nanocomposite, the C1s peak intensifies and an additional peak of N1s is observed, confirming successful incorporation on the nanoparticles. The Fe2p spectra for CF and CF/N-CQDs are depicted in Fig. 3a. Two identifiable peaks at approximately 711.5 eV and 724.94 eV correspond to Fe2p2/3 and Fe2p1/2, respectively. A satellite peak of Fe is also noted at 719.4 eV. Likewise, the peak at Fe2p2/3 and its satellite peaks show that Fe2+ oxide species are present, while the peak position at Fe2p1/2 shows that Fe3+ oxide species are present. The Co2p spectra for CF and CF/N-CQDs are illustrated in Fig. 3b. Two distinctive peaks at the range 776–782.3- eV and 791–795.8 eV are observed, corresponding to Co2p3/2 and Co2p1/2, respectively. Additionally, two satellite peaks accompany these characteristic peaks. The Co2p3/2 peak and its satellite peaks indicate the presence of Co2+ oxide species, whereas existence of Co3+ oxide species is affirmed by the character trait peak of Co2p1/2 and its satellite peaks [4]. Furthermore, the Fe2p and Co2p peaks in the CF/N-CQDs sample exhibit a subtle shift towards higher binding energies, indicating that there are chemical interactions between N-CQDs and CF composites, resulting in a shift in the chemical environment of the surface Fe and Co in CF/N-CQDs. Fig. 3d, shows the O 1s core-level spectrum and it may be decomposed into several main contributions, for CF, the peaks located at approximately 529.9 eV corresponding to the lattice oxygen in the Co/Fe‑oxygen framework, and the second at 531.7 eV attributed to the oxygen originating from the surface hydroxide and to oxygen peak. Moreover, O1s peak for N-CQDs exhibits two peaks at 531.1 and 532.4eV, which are attributed to CO and COH/COC, respectively. The O1s for the composite exhibited the combination of the two. The O1s differed slightly from pure CF and N-CQDs (1.4 eV and 0.25 eV). This shift suggests that the chemical environment of O1s in CF/N-CQDs has transitioned. Fig. 3e depicts the N1s spectrum, with the deconvoluted peaks at 399, 400, 401 eV that are accredited to graphitic N bonds NH, NC, and (N-(C)3), respectively [40]. Fig. 3f depicts the C1s spectrum of N-CQDs and CF/N-CQDs, and the deconvoluted peaks CC (graphite), CO, CO/CN, and OCO bonds are responsible for the typical peaks at 284.0, 285.5, 288.0, and 289.2 eV, indicating the presence of N-CQDs in the CF/N-CQDs. The change in binding energy simply illustrates the transition of photogenerated electrons from CF to N-CQDs, suggesting a strong electronic pairing between the two materials.

These nanomaterials were heated under a helium atmosphere from 0 to 800 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min. The TGA curve reveals that the thermal decomposition of nanoparticles occurs in three consecutive endothermic stages. The initial stage, occurring between 38 and 248 °C, is associated with the liberation of sorbed/bonded hydroxyl groups found on the sample's surface or the loss of structural water [41]. From the TGA curves, almost no weight loss of the pristine CF can be seen in the whole temperature range, suggesting the high thermal stability of the CF. However, an obvious weight loss of the CF/N-CQDs composite can be observed between 350 and 470 °C due to the burning of N-CQDs. The N-CQDs content in the CF/N-CQDs composite is deduced to be about 40 wt% (see Fig. 4 ).

The magnetic properties of the photocatalysts were measured by their respective hysteresis loops (Fig. 5a). The field-dependent magnetic curve shows negligible residual magnetization (Mr) and coercivity (Hc), showing the superparamagnetic behavior of the nanomaterials at room temperature. The saturation magnetization values of CF and CF/N-CQDs composites were obtained as 127.72 and 44.12 emu/g, respectively. The decrease in the saturation magnetization of the CF/N-CQDs composite was due to the introduction of nonmagnetic N-CQDs on the surface of the CF [42,43]. Nevertheless, the saturation magnetization values were sufficiently robust to facilitate the separation of the composites from the solution. Additionally, the coercivity (Hc) values for CF and CF/N-CQDs composites were found to be in the range of 182.85 to 725.73 Oe, notably lower than the reported coercivity values of CoFe2O4 NPs, which were in the range of 6090.83 Oe, [44] as shown in Table 1 . The reduced coercivity (Hc) values indicate that the CF and CF/N-CQDs composites can be magnetically reused without substantial aggregation. This property is advantageous for large-scale applications in photocatalysis. Zero-field-cooling (ZFC) and field-cooling (FC) susceptibility versus temperature, χ(T), curves of the CF and CF/N-CQDs particles are illustrated in Fig. 5b and c. In Fig. 5b, distinct irreversibility is consistently noted between the Zero Field Cooled (ZFC) and Field Cooled (FC) curves. The ZFC plots exhibit a pronounced peak at a critical temperature known as the blocking temperature (TB). Below TB, the ZFC values decline rapidly, whereas the FC values exhibit a slight increase. TB signifies the temperature threshold at which the magnetic anisotropy barrier is surpassed by thermal activation energy alone, leading to the relaxation of nanoparticles from the ferrimagnetic state to the superparamagnetic state [29]. The observed patterns in Zero Field Cooled (ZFC) and Field Cooled (FC) behaviours are characteristic of superparamagnetism in magnetic single domains. However, for CF/N-CQDs particles in Fig. 5c, shows a different curve and this can be attributed to the nonmagnetic material of the surface of the CF.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, EDX, TEM and BET analysis
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology and nanostructure of as-synthesized materials were characterized by SEM and TEM. The produced CF nanoparticles and CF/N-CQDs nanohybrid predominantly consisted of loosely agglomerated, sphere-like nanoparticles and were also characterized by type 4 BET-hysteresis loops as shown Fig. 6 and Table 1, suggesting that the nanomaterials were mesoporous. The 0.30 nm lattice fringe spacings of N-CQDs correspond to the (002) planes of graphene, and the d-spacing of 0.25 nm can be attributed to the (311) lattice plane of CoFe2O4, as shown in Fig. S2 [45,46]. Additional characterization through Energy Dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis, depicted in Fig. 6g, reveals the presence of Co, Fe, C, and O elements in the product. The detection of C is attributed to the use of carbon tape during the analysis. Fig. 6h displays a representative EDX spectra of the nanocomposite, and higher concentration of carbon and, additional presence of N element. Additionally, the EDX image of N-CQDs shows only three expected elements, C, N and O, implying that no impurities were found. Moreover Fig. S2 shows the EDX mapping of the composite, and all the expected elements were observed, however N element could not be observed. This is due to the low penetration power of X-rays that makes it challenging to detect and quantify light elements like nitrogen in thicker or denser samples.

The aqueous solution of NPX as the reactant (indicated by the black line at 0 min) exhibits a prominent absorbance peak at 230 nm and a faint band around 270 and 330 nm in Fig. 7a. These peaks unequivocally represent the characteristic bands of NPX [47]. The peak at 230 nm exhibited a reduction in intensity as the degradation reaction time advanced under the influence of the CF/N-CQDs photocatalyst. We explored an adsorption process that has the potential to notably impact degradation activities in diverse catalytic process. As illustrated in Fig. 7b, the quantity of NPX adsorbed on CF/N-CQDs was measured over a 20-minute period with constant stirring in the dark. The outcome reveals that the CF/N-CQDs catalyst demonstrated minimal adsorption of NPX (20 %). The photolysis, in the absence of a CF/N-CQDs catalyst, reached only 12 % of NPX degradation, and is insufficient to attain total photodegradation owing to the low rate of reactive oxygen species formation. From Fig. 7c, the photodegradations of NPX in different processes, the data of photodegradation reaction, conformed well to the first-order kinetic model. The slopes of the regression lines serve as indicators for the kinetic constants (k) in pseudo-first-order models. The k 1 values indicated that the degradation efficiency of NPX in the presence of CF/N-CQDs under visible photons radiation is 4.7, 2.6, 12.9, and 13.1 times greater than the CF, N-CQDs, adsorption, and photolysis, respectively. Specifically, this significant finding suggests that CF/N-CQDs hybrid catalysts hold greater promise for the degradation of NPX, with the degradation efficiency of 89.5 %.

Since the relationships between the catalyst surface, NPX, and reactive oxygen species differ with pH, the initial pH can have a major effect on the degradation of organic pollutants in aqueous solutions. The effect of solution pH (3.2–11) on photodegradation of NPX was explored to better understand the degradation process, shown in Fig. 8a. NPX is positively charged at pH (3.8) less than pKa (4.2), but negatively charged at pH greater than pKa attributable to carboxylic acid deprotonation [6]. The rate of NPX photodegradation enhanced in a neutral environment but decreased in an acidic or alkaline environment. Because NPX has a pKa of 4.15, it exists as a neutral molecule when pH is less than 4.15 and as an anionic molecule when pH is greater than 4.15. Based on the above results, the subsequent experiments were carried out under neutral pH conditions, which were determined to be the most cost-effective.

The findings presented in Fig. 8c demonstrate the impact of NPX's initial concentration (C0). When C0 is equal to or less than 5 mg/L, NPX can be completely removed within 60 min. However, when C0 exceeds 10 mg/L, NPX persists throughout the reaction period. This is attributed to the fact that photocatalysis predominantly takes place on the surface of photocatalysts, and at higher concentrations, NPX molecules occupy active sites, hindering photon absorption. Consequently, the efficiency of photodegradation is increasingly impeded with higher C0 values. Similar results were also observed by Fu et al. [48].

Tap water and various anions were tested to investigate the influence of various realistic water conditions on NPX photodegradation by the CF/N-CQDs nanocomposite (Fig. 8e and f). Anionic ions are commonly found in real-world water samples and can affect the photodegradation process [49]. Experiments were carried out in the presence of common anionic ions such as chloride, carbonates, and nitrate to assess the influence of anions on NPX photocatalytic degradation. NPX removal was reduced to 69.2 %, 59.0 %, and 71.6 % in the presence of chlorides, nitrate, and carbonates, as shown in Fig. 3. In the presence of chloride, the anions can react with OH to form Cl, thus slightly reducing the degradation efficiency of NPX, as shown in Eq. (4). The HCO3 − ions tend to generate CO3
− radical anions by consuming more active OH radicals (Eq. (5)) [50].

Furthermore, Eq. (6) shows nitrate ions can react with h+ via light absorption, reducing the degradation efficiency. These ions prevent NPX molecules from adsorbing to the photocatalyst surface, resulting in a decrease in catalyst activity. Tap water comprises of ions and other metals that distilled water does not contain. As shown in Fig. 8e and b, NPX degradation is reduced to some extent in tap water (TW). This is due to the fact that electrolytes in TW can interfere with the formation of reactive oxygen species and active sites [51].

ce:section.ce:section-title: The proposed degradation mechanism and the involvement of the reactive oxygen species
ce:section.ce:para: Various scavenging experiments with and without scavengers were carried out to ascertain the main mechanism in the process of NPX degradation under visible light irradiation (Fig. 9a). To identify the primary active groups participating in the photodegradation of organic pollutants, 5 mM isopropanol (quencher for OH), 5 mM formic acid (quencher for h+), and 5 mM ascorbic acid (quencher for O2 −) were introduced into the photocatalytic system as scavengers during the photocatalytic reaction. Isopropanol is a strong HO scavenger, with the reaction rate constants being k = 7 × 108 M−1 s−1. Nevertheless, the incorporation of isopropanol (ISO) does not result in any reduction in the degradation efficiency of NPX, signifying the absence of OH radicals in the degradation process. In contrast, the addition of formic acid (FA) and ascorbic acid (AA) significantly diminishes the degradation efficiency. Consequently, it can be deduced that h + and O2 − radicals are the active groups participating in the photodegradation of organic pollutants, with O2 − radicals playing a more pivotal role in the photodegradation reaction than the photogenerated holes (h+).

Additionally, the band edge positions (ECB and EVB) of the nanomaterials were calculated using Eqs. (7) and (8).
(7)
E CB
= χ –
E C
– 0 .
5E g
(8)

E CB
+
E g
where, χ is the absolute electronegativity of the NPs and it is characterized as the geometric mean of the absolute electronegativity of the principal atoms, which is described as the arithmetic average of the atomic electron affinity and first ionization energy. EC is the energy of free electrons on the normal hydrogen scale, NHE (~4.5 eV), and Eg is the energy band gap of the NPs [52]. Based on the experimental and XPS results, the possible pollutant degradation mechanism of the CF/N-CQDs photocatalytic system is proposed (Fig. 9b). In line with the band gap energy of CF, it has the capability to absorb and release electrons from its valence band (VB) into the conduction band (CB) when exposed to visible light. Furthermore, N-CQDs possess a lower unoccupied molecular orbital (LUMO) level than CF. Consequently, electrons will undergo additional transfer to N-CQDs, fostering the separation of charge carriers and thereby augmenting the photocatalytic capability for the target reaction [53,54]. Ultimately, these electrons are captured by O2 either at the photocatalyst's surface or within the solution, resulting in the formation of O2 − radicals or other reactive oxygen species, such as HO, facilitating the degradation of NPX molecules. As a result, the recombination of electron-hole pairs is impeded. Furthermore, the valence band (VB) of CF is situated at a higher energy level compared to that of H2O (E0 = OH/H2O = 2.68 V, vs. NHE) and OH− (E0 = OH/OH− = 1.99 V, vs. NHE). Therefore, the holes of CF cannot oxidize H2O and OH−, aligning with the findings of the aforementioned quenching experiments. Additionally, the holes (h+) generated below the Fermi level of CF have the capability to directly oxidize organic pollutants into smaller molecules.

One of the most significant advantages of using heterogeneous catalysts is their recovery and reusability. Because of the superparamagnetic properties present in the material at room temperature, the CF/N-CQDs studied in this study are easily recoverable from the reaction using a commercial magnet. After each treatment, the NC was recovered and reused in five consecutive cycles of treatment in presence. The NC showed a decrease in catalyst efficiency with subsequent uses as displayed in Fig. 9c. However, the loss of catalyst efficiency is insignificant. Moreover, TOC results also showed similar trends and mineralization of above 75 %. The FTIR spectra of samples, both freshly prepared and after undergoing five photocatalytic cycles, exhibited remarkable similarity (see Fig. S4). This suggests that the structure of the photocatalyst remained unchanged even after prolonged usage.

The CF/N-CQDs hybrid composite was created using simple and efficient microwave synthesis and N-CQDs were effectively anchored onto the cobalt ferrite surface. According to the various techniques used to characterize the results, the incorporation of N-CQDs onto the surface of cobalt ferrite has significantly increased visible light absorption when compared to pristine cobalt ferrite. XPS showed a strong electronic coupling between the CF and N-CQDs. Additionally, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of NPX rate can reach up to approximately 90 % within 2 h. Photoelectrochemical assessments provided evidence that the inclusion of CQDs (as electron acceptors) efficiently hinders the recombination of electrons (e−) and holes (h+) while facilitating charge transfer. The quenching outcomes indicated the participation of O2 −, h+, and HO in the photocatalytic degradation process. The active radicals were studied, and mechanism was also elucidated. N-CQDs have taken center stage as excellent electron transfer/reservoir for CF when exposed to visible light. This composite displayed great reusability in four cycles due to its ferromagnetic properties.
